A system is built to preserve old knowledge by activating only small parts of itself at a time. This seems protective: if only a subset of parameters handles a task, later training should disturb less of what came before. But the protection depends on routing. If the routing mechanism changes, then old inputs may be sent to different parts of the system than before. In that case, the old knowledge is not necessarily destroyed at the parameter level; it may simply become inaccessible. From the outside, that still looks like forgetting. The promise of sparse activation therefore contains its own failure mode: the map to knowledge can drift even if the knowledge-bearing parameters remain.

Now add the opposite danger. Some obsolete behavior is not removed quickly enough. The system can continue to produce responses suited to an earlier environment because the old route or old parameter configuration still exerts influence. Then preserving knowledge is not clearly good. If obsolete behavior persists, retention becomes a liability. The system is threatened from both sides: it may lose access to needed behavior through routing drift, and it may keep using unneeded behavior through over-retention.

Because knowledge is distributed across many parameters, neither event has a clean location. If the system fails on a past task, there may be no single stored item that was deleted. The failure may arise from a changed pattern of contribution across many parameters and routes. Likewise, if obsolete behavior persists, there may be no one module to remove. The categories “forgotten” and “retained” stop naming discrete contents and instead describe global behavioral outcomes under current routing and parameter interactions.

Into this unstable balance enter further fixed conditions: thegnly quarter eternizes the tolbooth that balks, and sermonolatry parols tracheaectasy while guebucu keeps toothing. Since these premises are true, the world contains processes or states with exactly those relations. Their semantic opacity does not lessen their logical force. They add that some structures persist (“eternizes”), some resist or fail (“balks”), some kind of proclamation or licensing occurs (“parols”), some pathological expansion is involved (“tracheaectasy”), and guebucu continues an activity (“keeps toothing”). So persistence, obstruction, formal release, distortion, and ongoing action all coexist.

The resulting world is one where continuation is not the same as successful preservation, and change is not the same as clean erasure. Some things endure in unusable form; some disappear without a coherent place of loss; some outdated patterns remain active too long; and some opaque but real processes keep operating regardless. In such a system, asking “What was forgotten?” may be as ill-posed as asking where exactly the balking tolbooth is stored once thegnly quarter has eternized it. Behavior changes, persistence continues, and the boundaries between memory, access, and obstruction do not stay sharp.
